Reviewer Bot Design The reviewer bot is a claim-level reviewer, not a paper-level executioner. For each comment it should record: - affected claim; - weak link; - evidence; - severity; - repair path; - whether the author accepted, rejected, or deferred the comment. The model should support strategic forgiveness. Not every imperfection is fatal; the point is to make the remaining imperfection inspectable.

Interactive Appendix Spec An interactive appendix should be a reader-facing inspection layer, not a product demo. Minimum interface: - search by claim ID; - filter by warrant level; - inspect source records; - inspect reviewer flags; - inspect uncertainty notes; - inspect relevant workflow scripts; - export a static Markdown view. The interface should preserve the PDF as a stable narrative object while making the evidence system queryable.

Transparency Log Appendix The transparency log records: - sources searched; - sources screened; - screening depth; - sources cited; - sources excluded; - exclusion reasons; - source-type breakdown; - discipline breakdown; - date distribution; - geography and language bias; - AI/tool use; - human audit steps; - unresolved uncertainty; - limitations of the literature sweep. See `evidence/transparency_log.yml`.

AI assistance was used to inspect the existing paper-pipeline architecture, browse and summarise current anchor sources, draft Python scripts, draft LaTeX and Markdown prose, generate workflow documentation, and run validation commands. Human responsibility remains central. The claim ledger, source interpretations, warrant levels, and article prose require human audit before submission or public release.

Git-Diff Voice Learning Accepted human edits should be treated as evidence of preference.

Workflow 1. The researcher edits prose directly in Overleaf. 2. The local repository pulls the Overleaf Git changes. 3. `git diff --unified=0` is inspected for prose changes. 4. Short before/after examples are extracted. 5. Candidate style rules are proposed. 6. The researcher accepts, rejects, or narrows the rules. 7. Future prose passes use accepted rules as suggestions only.

Current Rules - Preserve British English. - Prefer phenomenon-first claims over self-referential paper machinery. - Prefer direct mechanism formulations over loose not-only-but frames. - Reduce defensive caveats where a scope condition is clearer. - Preserve strong conceptual lines when they do analytical work. - Do not automatically overwrite prose based on inferred rules.

Example Before: > This paper argues that academic outputs should not only disclose AI use but also explain the workflow. After: > The article is the narrative layer of a wider evidence system. Candidate rule: > When the concept is strong enough, let it carry the sentence directly rather than framing it as a claim about what the paper does.

Human Audit Statement The required human audit before public release is: 1. Read the article for argument, tone, and overclaiming. 2. Check each cited source against the sentence-level claim it supports. 3. Review `evidence/claims.yml` and adjust warrant levels. 4. Review `evidence/sources.yml` for missing literatures and source bias. 5. Read `evidence/source_bias_report.md` and decide which blind spots need repair. 6. Compile the article and inspect the PDF. 7. Pull accepted Overleaf edits through Git and run the voice-learning process. 8. Accept, reject, or rewrite any proposed voice rules. The workflow is designed to make this audit easier. It does not replace it.

Reviewer Prompt Review the article as a metascience mechanism paper. Focus on bugs in logic, overclaiming, unsupported claims, missing governance literatures, weak mechanism analogies, excessive manifesto tone, and places where a whole-paper claim should become a claim-level warrant. For each issue, identify the affected claim, weak link, evidence, severity, and repair path.

Claim Extraction Prompt Read the article section files and extract sentence-level claims that do analytical work. For each claim, assign an ID, claim type, warrant level, source IDs, workflow links, uncertainty notes, and whether the claim is descriptive, conceptual, normative, speculative, or empirical. Do not treat polished prose as evidence. Every substantive claim needs a source, workflow warrant, or explicit uncertainty marker.

Literature Sweep Prompt Search for current and canonical work on AI research automation, research assessment reform, open science infrastructure, reproducibility, software citation, research objects, registered reports, p-hacking, many-analysts studies, publication bias, reviewer incentives, and AI authorship policies. For each source, record title, authors, year, venue, URL or DOI, source type, discipline, geography, language, screening depth, claims supported, claims challenged, notes, and exclusion reason if not retained.
